notes: Reconstructed from "ten".
Reconstructed from "sen".
Reconstructed from prefix "ú-".
Reconstructed from "phain".
Reconstructed from Gnomish "eleg".
Revised from Noldorin "tûg". Homonym to "tŷg" (thigh).
Word rejected by Tolkien, but no replacement was defined.
Reconstructed from "Mablung"
Reconstructed from "Caradras dilthen"

english: man, as a husband, partner, spouse

notes: From √ATATA, "two", "the second"
More formal than "bess", associated with "lady" as the wife of a "lord".
More formal than "benn" or "bethron", associated with "lord".
Standard word to describe any tree.

Word used for excurrent trees, that is, a tree with a strong central leader, giving it a tall, straight, relatively narrow form, such as pines, spruces, firs, poplars, and, to a lesser extent, birches. For decurrent trees, use the standard tree word "galadh".
Ancient variant plural of "orn" reinterpreted a collective word.
Probably a loanword from Nandorin

A neologism to describe a stick as a simple piece of wood, inspired by Germanic languages Stock, stok, stokk, Romance languages palus, pau, palo, par, Finnish keppi, Japanese 木の棒, Chinese 木棍. Conservative speakers may prefer to use "golf".
(There are two possible origins of this word, one is given below, and another one on Quettamorphosis.)
Reconstructed from "Fladrif".
Can refer to a single flower or a group of flowers.
Reconstructed from "Lisgardh".
Taken from "Taras-ness". The meaning is unclear, but it's assumed to mean "meadow" due to the possible root √NES.
Reconstructed from "talraph". The meaning has been theorised by David Salo. GS/281
Strong, grey and silky fibre made from the inner bark of mallorn trees.
Reconstructed from "Fladrif".
Neologism for "meat", based on Old English term "flesh-meat" (12c), in which "meat" referred to food in general. (French had a similar term, "viande", which meant "food".)
Adapted from Noldorin word "aes", which doesn't follow Sindarin phonetic standards. Some people use the Noldorin variant regardless.
Neologism coined based on Neo-Quenya word.
Reconstructed from "gwaelod".
hidden: Roots seem to work with law+tod

notes: This word predates "laud" because the latter was only published in 2024.

english: head of hair, fax, mass of long hair

notes: The singular form is based on a primitive dual inflection, and the plural is based on the ancient plural, so they don't follow standard pluralisation rules.
The singular form is based on a primitive dual inflection, and the plural is based on the ancient plural, so they don't follow standard pluralisation rules.

This word follows the same pattern of "lhaw" (ears) and "sunt" (nostrils), in which a primitive dual is fossilised into a modern Sindarin word. As a pair of eyes is as natural as a pair of ears or nostrils, it makes sense to have a dual plural for "eye".
